insights: All five departments (Database, Hardware, Inquiry/Help, Software, Network) achieve identical 100% goal achievement rates, confirming no department demonstrates elevated performance on this primary metric.

Each department handles exactly one category (e.g., Database group → Database category), eliminating cross-departmental comparability of work types and preventing identification of transferable operational practices.

52.8% of tickets exhibit negative resolution times (closed_at before opened_at), with systematic temporal clustering—rising from 7.3% in Jan 2023 to 97.1% in Jan 2024—indicating synthetic data generation artifacts rather than correctable data entry errors.

Resolution time variation across departments is statistically present but operationally meaningless: column-swap correction attempts yield 82.4% of tickets exceeding 30 days, confirming no reliable alternative performance metric can be derived from the current data.

The absence of performance differentials, structural segregation of work, and irrecoverable resolution time corruption collectively invalidate the premise that transferable goal management practices can be extracted from inter-departmental comparison in this dataset.

Temporal analysis confirms the data reflects algorithmic generation patterns—not real-world operational behavior—making trend-based or causal inferences about goal achievement or efficiency fundamentally unsupported.
summary: The investigation definitively refutes the premise of an 'elevated goal achievement department': all five departments achieve 100% goal completion with zero variance, eliminating any basis for comparative best-practice extraction. Structural constraints—perfect one-to-one mapping between departments and categories—prevent cross-functional learning, while systemic data corruption (52.8% negative resolution times, temporally escalating) renders alternative performance metrics unreliable and uncorrectable. These three barriers—performance parity, work-type segregation, and metric corruption—are mutually reinforcing and conclusively prevent the original goal of enhancing enterprise-wide goal management through inter-departmental benchmarking. Next steps must shift focus to data governance: auditing synthetic data generation protocols, redefining goal achievement metrics to align with verifiable timestamps, and collecting real-world operational data with validated time sequencing before attempting comparative analysis.
